infobox.value: ([Welsh](/wiki/Welsh_language "Welsh language"): *Clwb Rygbi Dreigiau*) are one of the four professional [rugby union](/wiki/Rugby_union "Rugby union") [regional teams in Wales](/wiki/Welsh_regional_rugby "Welsh regional rugby"). They play in the [United Rugby Championship](/wiki/United_Rugby_Championship "United Rugby Championship") and the [European Rugby Champions Cup](/wiki/European_Rugby_Champions_Cup "European Rugby Champions Cup")/[European Rugby Challenge Cup](/wiki/European_Rugby_Challenge_Cup "European Rugby Challenge Cup"). They play their home games at [Rodney Parade](/wiki/Rodney_Parade "Rodney Parade"), [Newport](/wiki/Newport,_Wales "Newport, Wales"), which was the home of Newport RFC from 1877 to 2017, and is shared with [Newport County AFC](/wiki/Newport_County_AFC "Newport County AFC"), the city's [English Football League](/wiki/English_Football_League "English Football League") team.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

**Dragons RFC** ([Welsh](/wiki/Welsh_language "Welsh language"): *Clwb Rygbi Dreigiau*) are one of the four professional [rugby union](/wiki/Rugby_union "Rugby union") [regional teams in Wales](/wiki/Welsh_regional_rugby "Welsh regional rugby"). They play in the [United Rugby Championship](/wiki/United_Rugby_Championship "United Rugby Championship") and the [European Rugby Champions Cup](/wiki/European_Rugby_Champions_Cup "European Rugby Champions Cup")/[European Rugby Challenge Cup](/wiki/European_Rugby_Challenge_Cup "European Rugby Challenge Cup"). They play their home games at [Rodney Parade](/wiki/Rodney_Parade "Rodney Parade"), [Newport](/wiki/Newport,_Wales "Newport, Wales"), which was the home of Newport RFC from 1877 to 2017, and is shared with [Newport County AFC](/wiki/Newport_County_AFC "Newport County AFC"), the city's [English Football League](/wiki/English_Football_League "English Football League") team.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

The Dragons were formed in 2003 as a result of the [introduction of regional rugby union teams in Wales](/wiki/Introduction_of_regional_rugby_union_teams_in_Wales "Introduction of regional rugby union teams in Wales"), and represent a region that covers an area of southeast Wales including [Blaenau Gwent](/wiki/Blaenau_Gwent "Blaenau Gwent"), [Caerphilly](/wiki/Caerphilly "Caerphilly"), [Monmouthshire](/wiki/Monmouthshire "Monmouthshire"), Newport and [Torfaen](/wiki/Torfaen "Torfaen"), with a total population approaching 600,000. They are affiliated with a number of semi-professional and amateur clubs throughout the area, including [Pontypool RFC](/wiki/Pontypool_RFC "Pontypool RFC"), [Caerphilly RFC](/wiki/Caerphilly_RFC "Caerphilly RFC"), [Cross Keys RFC](/wiki/Cross_Keys_RFC "Cross Keys RFC"), [Ebbw Vale RFC](/wiki/Ebbw_Vale_RFC "Ebbw Vale RFC") and [Newport RFC](/wiki/Newport_RFC "Newport RFC").

The team has been owned by Dragons RFC Ltd since 2023, having been solely owned by the [Welsh Rugby Union](/wiki/Welsh_Rugby_Union "Welsh Rugby Union") from 2017 to 2023 and jointly owned by the WRU and [Newport RFC](/wiki/Newport_RFC "Newport RFC") between 2004 and 2017.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

The Dragons started life with a third-place finish in the 2003–04 [Celtic League](/wiki/United_Rugby_Championship "United Rugby Championship"), and finished fourth the next season; however, the team finished in the bottom three in each of the next four seasons.They reached the semi-finals of the European Challenge Cup on three occasions, in 2007,2015 and 2016. In 2011, they reached the semi-finals of the Anglo-Welsh Cup, losing to Gloucester. They are yet to make the knock-out stage of the [European Rugby Champions Cup](/wiki/European_Rugby_Champions_Cup "European Rugby Champions Cup").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Dragons_RFC&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

|  |
| --- |
| Cardiff Blues  Cardiff  Scarlets  Llanelli  Ospreys  Swansea  Neath  Dragons  Newport  The 4 Welsh Rugby Union Regions of Wales |

### Formation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Dragons_RFC&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Formation")]

The regional team were formed on 1 April 2003, following an agreement between [Ebbw Vale RFC](/wiki/Ebbw_Vale_RFC "Ebbw Vale RFC") and [Newport RFC](/wiki/Newport_RFC "Newport RFC") to form one of five regional rugby entities.[[3]](#cite_note-3) Fundamental disagreements between the clubs[[4]](#cite_note-4) saw a period of arbitration, led by the then [Welsh Rugby Union](/wiki/Welsh_Rugby_Union "Welsh Rugby Union") chief executive [David Moffett](/wiki/David_Moffett "David Moffett"), which recommended the name "Gwent Dragons". On 28 July the side was launched under that name.[[5]](#cite_note-5) This prompted [Newport RFC](/wiki/Newport_RFC "Newport RFC") benefactor Tony Brown of [Bisley](/wiki/Bisley,_Surrey "Bisley, Surrey"), Surrey to withdraw his financial support for the region. However, by 21 August Brown returned after Ebbw Vale chairman Marcus Russell resigned and the side's name was changed to "Newport and Gwent Dragons".[[6]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-6) With the Welsh Rugby Union demanding an explanation for the changes, and acrimony between the two clubs[[7]](#cite_note-7) another agreement was struck:[[8]](#cite_note-8) the side officially became 'Newport Gwent Dragons'. On 12 November 2003, the region's founding company Gwent Rugby Ltd entered into
administration.[[9]](#cite_note-9) On 27 November a new company, Dragons Rugby Ltd., was established to run the region, with Newport RFC and the Welsh Rugby Union each holding a 50% stake.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

### 2003–2005: Infancy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Dragons_RFC&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 2003–2005: Infancy")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/3/39/Newport_gwent_dragons_badge.png/250px-Newport_gwent_dragons_badge.png)](/wiki/File:Newport_gwent_dragons_badge.png)

The logo used by the regional team between 2003 and 2017.

Under [Mike Ruddock](/wiki/Mike_Ruddock "Mike Ruddock") and his assistant [Clive Griffiths](/wiki/Clive_Griffiths_(rugby) "Clive Griffiths (rugby)") Newport Gwent Dragons, with a squad largely drawn from the [Newport RFC](/wiki/Newport_RFC "Newport RFC") and [Ebbw Vale RFC](/wiki/Ebbw_Vale_RFC "Ebbw Vale RFC") sides of the preceding year, beat their limited pre-season expectations. Despite starting their life in top-class rugby with a 35–11 defeat away to [Llanelli Scarlets](/wiki/Scarlets "Scarlets"), it was the region's most successful season so far. A 29–19 win over the [Ospreys](/wiki/Ospreys_(rugby_union) "Ospreys (rugby union)") was to prove more telling; captained by [Andy Marinos](/wiki/Andy_Marinos "Andy Marinos") the side remained unbeaten at home in the Celtic League and eliminated [Stade Français](/wiki/Stade_Fran%C3%A7ais "Stade Français")[[11]](#cite_note-auto1-11) in the [Heineken Cup](/wiki/Heineken_Cup "Heineken Cup"). Going into the final round with an outside chance of taking the title, the Dragons finished third in the Celtic League[[12]](#cite_note-autogenerated2-12) [WRU](/wiki/Welsh_Rugby_Union "Welsh Rugby Union") bosses were impressed enough to appoint Ruddock to the vacant [Welsh](/wiki/Wales_national_rugby_union_team "Wales national rugby union team") coaching job in summer 2004.[[13]](#cite_note-13) In 2005, Ruddock guided Wales to a Grand Slam Title in the Six Nations. Ruddock rewarded two of his former Dragons players, [Hal Luscombe](/wiki/Hal_Luscombe "Hal Luscombe") and [Jason Forster](/wiki/Jason_Forster "Jason Forster"), with their first test caps on Wales' summer tour of [Argentina](/wiki/Argentina_national_rugby_union_team "Argentina national rugby union team"). Wales [Percy Montgomery](/wiki/Percy_Montgomery "Percy Montgomery") also impressed Springbok selectors enough to remind them of his international credentials, and earn a [Tri Nations](/wiki/Tri_Nations_(rugby_union) "Tri Nations (rugby union)") recall.

The following off season saw a marked change in direction. [Gareth Cooper](/wiki/Gareth_Cooper "Gareth Cooper"), [Kevin Morgan](/wiki/Kevin_Morgan_(rugby_union) "Kevin Morgan (rugby union)") and [Ceri Sweeney](/wiki/Ceri_Sweeney "Ceri Sweeney") were amongst a handful of players who joined the region when the [Celtic Warriors](/wiki/Celtic_Warriors "Celtic Warriors") were disbanded. Having originally agreed to replace Mike Ruddock as head coach, [Declan Kidney](/wiki/Declan_Kidney "Declan Kidney") decided instead to seek employment back home with [Leinster](/wiki/Leinster_RFC "Leinster RFC"). It was not until 27 July 2004 that former [Australia national rugby league team](/wiki/Australia_national_rugby_league_team "Australia national rugby league team") coach [Chris Anderson](/wiki/Chris_Anderson_(rugby_league) "Chris Anderson (rugby league)") was appointed, with Leigh Jones as his assistant. Another credible Celtic League campaign followed, finishing fourth,[[12]](#cite_note-autogenerated2-12) the second highest Welsh region. The side's Heineken Cup could be viewed as a wasted opportunity: the team beat French side [Perpignan](/wiki/USA_Perpignan "USA Perpignan") 27–14 at home, but were then beaten home and away by [Newcastle Falcons](/wiki/Newcastle_Falcons "Newcastle Falcons") to put paid to any quarter-final ambitions. Chris Anderson's contract was not extended beyond its initial one-year duration.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

### 2005–2011 Paul Turner era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Dragons_RFC&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 2005–2011 Paul Turner era")]

The region looked to [Harlequins](/wiki/Harlequin_F.C. "Harlequin F.C.") backs coach [Paul Turner](/wiki/Paul_Turner_(rugby_union) "Paul Turner (rugby union)"), a Welshman, as their new head coach.[[15]](#cite_note-15) Turner would also have to contend with [Percy Montgomery](/wiki/Percy_Montgomery "Percy Montgomery") returning to South Africa[[16]](#cite_note-16) and Newport RFC stalwart [Rod Snow](/wiki/Rod_Snow "Rod Snow") retiring.[[17]](#cite_note-17) [Munster](/wiki/Munster_rugby "Munster rugby") and [Sale Sharks](/wiki/Sale_Sharks "Sale Sharks") proved too strong in the [2005–06 Heineken Cup](/wiki/2005%E2%80%9306_Heineken_Cup "2005–06 Heineken Cup"). After finishing 8th in the Celtic League, a 24–15 defeat[[18]](#cite_note-18) to [Overmach Parma](/wiki/Overmach_Parma "Overmach Parma") in a play-off for a place in the following seasons Heineken cup proved a new low for the region. Anglo-Welsh Cup wins over [Leicester Tigers](/wiki/Leicester_Tigers "Leicester Tigers") and [Northampton Saints](/wiki/Northampton_Saints "Northampton Saints") were the highlights of a tough season. Turner remained, but Wales international [Hal Luscombe](/wiki/Hal_Luscombe "Hal Luscombe") opted for a move away from the region, joining English Premiership side Harlequins.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

Former Wales captain [Colin Charvis](/wiki/Colin_Charvis "Colin Charvis") joined ahead of the 2006–07 season with the Dragons progressed into a [European Challenge Cup](/wiki/European_Challenge_Cup "European Challenge Cup") semi-final, where they lost comfortably to [ASM Clermont Auvergne](/wiki/Association_Sportive_Montferrandaise_Clermont_Auvergne "Association Sportive Montferrandaise Clermont Auvergne"). Domestically though, the region's European exploits appeared to take their toll, finishing ninth in the Celtic League. Significantly the region avoided the prospect of a second season away from the Heineken Cup, defeating another Italian side [Calvisano](/wiki/Rugby_Calvisano "Rugby Calvisano") 22–15.[[20]](#cite_note-20) The match also marked the end for departing Wales internationals [Ian Gough](/wiki/Ian_Gough "Ian Gough") and [Gareth Cooper](/wiki/Gareth_Cooper "Gareth Cooper") at Rodney Parade.

2007–08 proved to be another difficult season for the region. Signings such as scrum-half [Andy Williams](/wiki/Andy_Williams_(rugby_union) "Andy Williams (rugby union)") and flanker [Richard Parks](/wiki/Richard_Parks "Richard Parks") were not able to help reverse the side's fortunes. The Dragons [2007–08 Heineken Cup](/wiki/2007%E2%80%9308_Heineken_Cup "2007–08 Heineken Cup") campaign only saw one win against Italian side [Treviso](/wiki/Benetton_Rugby "Benetton Rugby") and exiting the Anglo-Welsh Cup in the pool stages for a third year running. Between completing a double over [Llanelli Scarlets](/wiki/Scarlets "Scarlets") on 1 January[[21]](#cite_note-21) to defeating the Ospreys on 6 May,[[22]](#cite_note-autogenerated3-22) the Dragons failed to win a Celtic League game. Despite finishing as the lowest-placed Welsh side in the league[[23]](#cite_note-23) the region qualified for next season's Heineken Cup, without having to play off against Italian opposition due to a failure by the Italian League to finish before a specified date.[[11]](#cite_note-auto1-11)

The summer of 2008 marked a change in the Dragons recruitment policy to a more [antipodean](/wiki/Antipodes "Antipodes") focus.[[24]](#cite_note-24) Several new signings included New Zealander Tom Willis who was also appointed captain.[[25]](#cite_note-25) The 2008–09 Heineken Cup saw visible signs of encouragement for the region. An opening round defeat of Glasgow at Rodney Parade and two respectable defeats to French giants Toulouse, sandwiched between narrow losses against Bath offered hope of arresting decline at Rodney Parade. Domestically in the Celtic League it was a case of same old as consecutive defeats in rearranged matches away at Cardiff Blues and the Ospreys ended any hopes of avoiding finishing as the lowest placed Welsh region and another Heineken Cup play off against Italian opposition. In record appearance holder Adam Black's final game for the side, the Dragons ran out comfortable winners away to Calvisano[[26]](#cite_note-26) to secure their place in European rugby's premier tournament for a third season running.

The 2009–10 season brought about significant improvements in results, with the Dragons remaining unbeaten at Rodney Parade in the Celtic League until their final home match, a 20–14 loss to Cardiff Blues.[[27]](#cite_note-27) Defeat also brought about the end of the Dragons bid to qualify for the inaugural Celtic League playoffs. An improved seventh-place finish did, however, mean automatic qualification for the Heineken cup as the third highest finishing Welsh region. Despite coming close to defeating Gloucester away at Kingsholm and a win at home against Glasgow, back to back losses at Biarritz put paid to the Dragons chances of progressing past the group stages of the Heineken Cup for the first time.

### 2011–2017

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Dragons_RFC&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 2011–2017")]

Turner stepped down as head coach in February 2011 with [Darren Edwards](/wiki/Darren_Edwards "Darren Edwards") taking over in a caretaker capacity.[[28]](#cite_note-28) In March 2011 Edwards led the Dragons to their first Anglo-Welsh Cup semi-final, where they lost to Gloucester. In April 2011 Edwards was appointed Head Coach on a full-time basis. [Lyn Jones](/wiki/Lyn_Jones "Lyn Jones") was appointed to the role of Director of Rugby in 2013 taking over a lot of on field responsibilities.[[29]](#cite_note-29) He brought with him then [Russia](/wiki/Russia_national_rugby_union_team "Russia national rugby union team") head coach [Kingsley Jones](/wiki/Kingsley_Jones_(rugby_union,_born_1969) "Kingsley Jones (rugby union, born 1969)") who worked with him as a consultant at [London Welsh](/wiki/London_Welsh_RFC "London Welsh RFC").[[30]](#cite_note-30) Edwards left the Dragons in February 2014[[31]](#cite_note-31) while in June, Jones was promoted to the role of head coach.[[32]](#cite_note-32)

During the 2014–2015 season, the Dragons reached the semi-finals of the [Challenge Cup](/wiki/Challenge_Cup "Challenge Cup"), having defeated the Cardiff Blues 25–21.[[33]](#cite_note-33) This was their second time reaching the semi-finals, having done so in 2007. They lost 45–16 to Edinburgh in the semi-final.[[34]](#cite_note-34)

The following season, the Dragons once again reached the semi-finals of the Challenge Cup, but lost 22–12 to [Montpellier](/wiki/Montpellier_H%C3%A9rault_Rugby "Montpellier Hérault Rugby").[[35]](#cite_note-35)

### 2017–2023: WRU ownership

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Dragons_RFC&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 2017–2023: WRU ownership")]

In March 2017, following a vote of [Newport RFC](/wiki/Newport_RFC "Newport RFC") shareholders, the [Welsh Rugby Union](/wiki/Welsh_Rugby_Union "Welsh Rugby Union") agreed to take over the Newport Gwent Dragons in its entirety as part of a deal that also saw the WRU take ownership of the [Rodney Parade](/wiki/Rodney_Parade "Rodney Parade") ground.[[36]](#cite_note-36) Following the takeover, [Bernard Jackman](/wiki/Bernard_Jackman "Bernard Jackman") was appointed head coach, and on 20 June 2017 it was announced that following the takeover of the region by the WRU, the region would be dropping "Newport Gwent" from its name with immediate effect, becoming known simply as "Dragons".[[37]](#cite_note-37) Jackman's endured a difficult first season in charge in 2017–18, with the Dragons recording only two wins in the league. Despite recruiting heavily for the 2018-19 campaign, including Wales forwards [Ross Moriarty](/wiki/Ross_Moriarty "Ross Moriarty") and [Richard Hibbard](/wiki/Richard_Hibbard "Richard Hibbard"), results did not improve and Jackman was dismissed in 2019 to be replaced by [Dean Ryan](/wiki/Dean_Ryan "Dean Ryan").

During the WRU-ownership period, the Dragons signed a large number of English-born players eligible for Wales thanks to parents or grandparents, such as [Ross Moriarty](/wiki/Ross_Moriarty "Ross Moriarty"), [Will Talbot-Davies](/wiki/Will_Talbot-Davies "Will Talbot-Davies"), [Tom Griffiths](/wiki/Tom_Griffiths_(rugby_union) "Tom Griffiths (rugby union)"), [Huw Taylor](/wiki/Huw_Taylor "Huw Taylor"), [Nick Tompkins](/wiki/Nick_Tompkins "Nick Tompkins"), [Joe Maksymiw](/wiki/Joe_Maksymiw "Joe Maksymiw"), [Greg Bateman](/wiki/Greg_Bateman "Greg Bateman") and [Will Rowlands](/wiki/Will_Rowlands "Will Rowlands").

Ahead of the 2022–2023 season, the Dragons continued their extensive recruitment drive, including established props [Rhodri Jones](/wiki/Rhodri_Jones "Rhodri Jones") and [Rob Evans](/wiki/Rob_Evans_(rugby_union) "Rob Evans (rugby union)"), as well as locks [Sean Lonsdale](/wiki/Sean_Lonsdale "Sean Lonsdale") and [George Nott](/wiki/George_Nott "George Nott") from the Premiership. [JJ Hanrahan](/wiki/JJ_Hanrahan "JJ Hanrahan") joined from Clermont, while two centres; Welsh-qualified [Max Clark](/wiki/Max_Clark_(rugby_union) "Max Clark (rugby union)") and New Zealander [Sio Tomkinson](/wiki/Sio_Tomkinson "Sio Tomkinson") added further depth. Further signings were made in the lead up to season.

### 2023 onwards: Return to private ownership

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Dragons_RFC&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 2023 onwards: Return to private ownership")]

In July 2023 a [consortium](/wiki/Consortium "Consortium") headed by Dragons chairman [David Buttress](/wiki/Just_Eat "Just Eat") bought Rodney Parade and the Dragons from the WRU.[[38]](#cite_note-38)

During the [2024–25 United Rugby Championship](/wiki/2024%E2%80%9325_United_Rugby_Championship "2024–25 United Rugby Championship"), the Dragons saw their worst season yet, with one win in the league, equaling the league record.[[39]](#cite_note-39) During the season, head coach Dai Flanagan was sacked and replaced by defence coach [Filo Tiatia](/wiki/Filo_Tiatia "Filo Tiatia"), however little improvement was seen, with the only subsequent win coming in the [EPCR Challenge Cup](/wiki/EPCR_Challenge_Cup "EPCR Challenge Cup") against [Newcastle Falcons](/wiki/Newcastle_Falcons "Newcastle Falcons").[[40]](#cite_note-40)

## Team name

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Dragons_RFC&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Team name")]

The naming of the region's team caused considerable turbulence.[[41]](#cite_note-41) Newport Gwent Dragons were a new side created out of the restructuring of Welsh rugby, and represent their designated region, like the [Cardiff Blues](/wiki/Cardiff_Blues "Cardiff Blues"), the [Scarlets](/wiki/Scarlets "Scarlets") and the [Ospreys](/wiki/Ospreys_(rugby_union) "Ospreys (rugby union)"). Some in the Welsh rugby world, such as [Bobby Windsor](/wiki/Bobby_Windsor "Bobby Windsor"), believed that including the name Newport would alienate some fans in the surrounding valleys.[[42]](#cite_note-42) Many supporters in the wider Newport area favoured greater identification with the City of Newport and a continuation of the historic traditions of Newport RFC.[[6]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-6) Several names were suggested but all were rejected by the WRU. In the end, the WRU decided the region would be called the Gwent Dragons. However, initial response to the new region was mixed, with many fans unsure whether to buy a season ticket for the new side or to stick to their local clubs.[[43]](#cite_note-43) The company set up to run the side entered administration before a game had been played, and as a compromise the word "Newport" was added to the team name in a double-sized font, whilst "Gwent" was reduced. This addition and choice of kit added a greater Newport emphasis to the region and polarised the regions' fan base: some supporters of Ebbw Vale, Pontypool, Cross Keys and Newbridge turned their backs on the regional side, claiming that Gwent was no longer being equally represented.[[44]](#cite_note-44) This debate continued, with the Dragons being accused of favouritism towards their Newport feeder club rather than the other feeder clubs.[[45]](#cite_note-45)

The Newport Action Group, among others, claimed the side has lost more supporters by including the name "Gwent" in its title. The crowds supporting Newport Gwent Dragons averaged 5,154 for the 2005–06 season,[[46]](#cite_note-46) whereas in the 2002–03 season, Newport RFC was Wales' best supported club and British rugby's fourth best with an average attendance of 8,302 – behind English Premiership clubs [Leicester](/wiki/Leicester_Tigers "Leicester Tigers"), [Gloucester](/wiki/Gloucester_Rugby "Gloucester Rugby") and [Northampton](/wiki/Northampton_Saints "Northampton Saints").[[47]](#cite_note-47) In the 2006–07 season, attendance averaged 5,629 at Rodney Parade.

In 2017, after the takeover by the WRU, the club was rebranded as simply “The Dragons”, omitting any geographic identifier.[[48]](#cite_note-48)

On the 27 June 2022, the Dragons announced their rebranding as Dragons RFC. The new playing colours being black and amber to represent the traditional colours of Newport sporting teams and the blue of the historic [Monmouthshire](/wiki/Monmouthshire_(historic) "Monmouthshire (historic)") county. The new badge includes three [fleur-de-lis](/wiki/Fleur-de-lis "Fleur-de-lis") derived from the county [Flag of Monmouthshire](/wiki/Flag_of_Monmouthshire "Flag of Monmouthshire").[[49]](#cite_note-49)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Dragons_RFC&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Kit")]

The Dragons kit for the 2022–2023 season is supplied by [VX3](/w/index.php?title=VX3_(sports_equipment)&action=edit&redlink=1 "VX3 (sports equipment) (page does not exist)"). Their current 'Headline Sponsor' is Dragon Signs.

## Esports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Dragons_RFC&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Esports")]

Dragons RFC established an esports team in 2023 to compete in the Welsh Esports League (WEL) after the league was launched that year.[[50]](#cite_note-50)[[51]](#cite_note-51) Among the founding teams were Dragons RFC Esports, Cardiff Pirates (linked to [Cardiff Rugby](/wiki/Cardiff_Rugby "Cardiff Rugby")), and the Pit Ponies (associated with [Pontypridd United F.C.](/wiki/Pontypridd_United_F.C. "Pontypridd United F.C.")), as reported by local media.[[52]](#cite_note-52)

In 2025, Dragons RFC fielded a *[Counter-Strike 2](/wiki/Counter-Strike_2 "Counter-Strike 2")* team in the Welsh Esports League Premiership. Their fixture against S.E.A. Dragons attracted notable regional coverage, in which Dragons lost 13–4.[[53]](#cite_note-53)[[54]](#cite_note-54) The team is also recognised as a full member club of Esports Wales.[[55]](#cite_note-ESWClubs-55)

## Home ground

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Dragons_RFC&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Home ground")]

The region's ground is the 8,700 capacity [Rodney Parade](/wiki/Rodney_Parade "Rodney Parade") ground in Newport, where they play the majority of their home games. Games are occasionally hosted at other grounds in Gwent, such as [Pontypool Park](/wiki/Pontypool_Park "Pontypool Park")[[56]](#cite_note-56) or Pandy Park (home of [Cross Keys RFC](/wiki/Cross_Keys_RFC "Cross Keys RFC")).[[57]](#cite_note-57) These are usually pre-season or other fixtures, however occasionally league games are taken elsewhere such as in 2017 when a game against local rivals [Cardiff Blues](/wiki/Cardiff_Blues "Cardiff Blues") was hosted at the Constructaquote Stadium (formerly [Virginia Park](/wiki/Virginia_Park_(Caerphilly) "Virginia Park (Caerphilly)")), home of [Caerphilly RFC](/wiki/Caerphilly_RFC "Caerphilly RFC"), due to a fixture clash with [Newport County AFC](/wiki/Newport_County_AFC "Newport County AFC");[[58]](#cite_note-auto-58) and during the 2017/18 season when the Dragons hosted a [Pro14](/wiki/United_Rugby_Championship "United Rugby Championship") game against [Edinburgh Rugby](/wiki/Edinburgh_Rugby "Edinburgh Rugby") in [Eugene Cross Park](/wiki/Eugene_Cross_Park "Eugene Cross Park"), Ebbw Vale.[[58]](#cite_note-auto-58)

As a part of [Judgement Day](/wiki/Judgement_Day_(rugby_union) "Judgement Day (rugby union)"), each season a home game against a rival Welsh rugby region is hosted at the [Millennium Stadium](/wiki/Millennium_Stadium "Millennium Stadium") in Cardiff.

In preparation for the 2014–15 season the Newport Gwent Dragons agreed a partnership with [Caerphilly County Borough Council](/wiki/Caerphilly_County_Borough_Council "Caerphilly County Borough Council") for the team and coaching staff to use the [CCB Centre for Sporting Excellence](/wiki/CCB_Centre_for_Sporting_Excellence "CCB Centre for Sporting Excellence") as the new training base for the 1st team and all other age grade structures within the region.[[59]](#cite_note-59) The small stadium at the centre hosts the home matches of the Dragons U23 side, which competes in the [Celtic Cup](/wiki/Celtic_Cup_(2018_rugby_union_tournament) "Celtic Cup (2018 rugby union tournament)"). Owing to a frozen pitch at Rodney Parade, the CCB Centre hosted a Challenge Cup fixture on 22 January 2023.[[60]](#cite_note-60)

### Notable players

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Dragons_RFC&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Notable players")]

See also: [Category:Dragons RFC players](/wiki/Category:Dragons_RFC_players "Category:Dragons RFC players")

Michael Owen captained [Wales](/wiki/Wales_national_rugby_union_team "Wales national rugby union team") in 2005–06 and he led Wales to their first [Grand Slam](/wiki/Grand_Slam_(Rugby_Union) "Grand Slam (Rugby Union)") for 27-years in the [2005 Six Nations Championship](/wiki/2005_Six_Nations_Championship "2005 Six Nations Championship").

[Lewis Evans](/wiki/Lewis_Evans_(rugby_union) "Lewis Evans (rugby union)") has made over 200 appearances for the Dragons. Prop [Adam Black](/wiki/Adam_Black_(rugby_union) "Adam Black (rugby union)") became the first centurion in Dragons colours during the 2006–07 season. The following players have also made over one hundred Dragons appearances: [Jamie Ringer](/wiki/Jamie_Ringer "Jamie Ringer"), [Peter Sidoli](/wiki/Peter_Sidoli "Peter Sidoli"), [Gareth Wyatt](/wiki/Gareth_Wyatt "Gareth Wyatt"), [Steve Jones](/wiki/Steve_Jones_(rugby_union,_born_1977) "Steve Jones (rugby union, born 1977)"), [Luke Charteris](/wiki/Luke_Charteris "Luke Charteris"), [Wayne Evans](/wiki/Wayne_Evans_(rugby_union) "Wayne Evans (rugby union)"), [Aled Brew](/wiki/Aled_Brew "Aled Brew"), [Ashley Smith](/wiki/Ashley_Smith_(rugby_union) "Ashley Smith (rugby union)"), [Adam Jones](/wiki/Adam_Jones_(rugby_union,_born_1980) "Adam Jones (rugby union, born 1980)"), [Hugh Gustafson](/wiki/Hugh_Gustafson "Hugh Gustafson"), [Jason Tovey](/wiki/Jason_Tovey "Jason Tovey"), [Robert Sidoli](/wiki/Robert_Sidoli "Robert Sidoli"), [Phil Price](/wiki/Phil_Price_(rugby_union) "Phil Price (rugby union)"), [Adam Hughes](/wiki/Adam_Hughes_(rugby_union,_born_1990) "Adam Hughes (rugby union, born 1990)"), [Nic Cudd](/wiki/Nic_Cudd "Nic Cudd"), [Rynard Landman](/wiki/Ligtoring_Landman "Ligtoring Landman"), [Matthew Screech](/wiki/Matthew_Screech "Matthew Screech"), [Brok Harris](/wiki/Brok_Harris "Brok Harris"), [Lloyd Fairbrother](/wiki/Lloyd_Fairbrother "Lloyd Fairbrother"), [Jack Dixon](/wiki/Jack_Dixon "Jack Dixon"), [Elliot Dee](/wiki/Elliot_Dee "Elliot Dee"), [Ashton Hewitt](/wiki/Ashton_Hewitt "Ashton Hewitt"), [Joe Davies](/wiki/Joe_Davies_(rugby_player) "Joe Davies (rugby player)"), [Harrison Keddie](/wiki/Harrison_Keddie "Harrison Keddie"), [Rhodri Williams](/wiki/Rhodri_Williams_(rugby_player) "Rhodri Williams (rugby player)"), [Ollie Griffiths](/wiki/Ollie_Griffiths "Ollie Griffiths"), [Aaron Wainwright](/wiki/Aaron_Wainwright "Aaron Wainwright").

| [2017–18](/wiki/2017%E2%80%9318_Anglo-Welsh_Cup "2017–18 Anglo-Welsh Cup") | 1 | 4th | 4 | 2 | 0 | 2 | 1 | 9 |

1. **[^](#cite_ref-65)** 11 teams were involved in this season, so one team did not play each week and were awarded four points instead. Therefore, each team finished the season with eight more points than the table would seem to warrant.
2. **[^](#cite_ref-66)** The competition was split into two conferences of 7 teams each following the increase from 12 to 14 teams.
3. **[^](#cite_ref-68)** Only 15 rounds were played during the [2019–20](/wiki/2019%E2%80%9320_Pro14 "2019–20 Pro14") season due to the [COVID-19 pandemic in Europe](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_Europe "COVID-19 pandemic in Europe").[[64]](#cite_note-behind_closed_doors-67)
4. **[^](#cite_ref-70)** Only 16 rounds were played during the [2020–21](/wiki/2020%E2%80%9321_Pro14 "2020–21 Pro14") season due to the [COVID-19 pandemic in Europe](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_Europe "COVID-19 pandemic in Europe"), with the remaining matches replaced by the [Pro14 Rainbow Cup](/wiki/Pro14_Rainbow_Cup "Pro14 Rainbow Cup") competition.[[65]](#cite_note-69)
5. **[^](#cite_ref-71)** Due to the [COVID-19 pandemic in Europe](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_Europe "COVID-19 pandemic in Europe"), only two pool games were played during the [2020–21](/wiki/2020%E2%80%9321_European_Rugby_Champions_Cup "2020–21 European Rugby Champions Cup") season. The knockout stage commenced with a round of 16 consisting of the top eight teams from each pool.
